Andreia, the rollout is done. Could you give the team some constructive feedback?
Of course. First, let me praise a real strength: your communication was excellent.
Good. And what would you like them to improve?
From my perspective, you could have explained the risk earlier. That is my one suggestion.
That is fair. How will you address it and stay honest but kind?
I will acknowledge their effort and use a simple framework: strengths first, then one suggestion.
One of your strengths is clear communication.
May I make a small suggestion to improve it?
Thank you. I acknowledge your point and I will work on it.
I will be honest but kind: strengths first, then one suggestion.

Hi Andreia, David Lewis here. Before the feedback meeting, let me brief you on how to give good feedback to the team. The rollout is done, and overall the result is positive, so start with praise. Name a real strength first, something the team genuinely did well, like their clear communication. People listen better when they feel acknowledged. Then, and only then, give the difficult part, but keep it soft. Use past modals: you could have explained the risk earlier, or we should have tested the console first. These forms point to a better past choice politely, without blaming anyone. Give one clear suggestion, not a long list. Use a simple framework: strengths first, then one improvement. End by being honest but kind, and acknowledge their perspective. Remember, the goal is to help the team improve, not to win an argument. Call me after the meeting. Thank you, Andreia.

Good morning, everyone, and thank you for the hard work on the console rollout. I would like to give you some constructive feedback, and I will use a simple framework: strengths first, then one suggestion. Let me start with praise. One of your biggest strengths was clear communication. You kept everyone informed, and that made my job much easier. Now, from my perspective, there is one thing we could improve. You could have explained the risk to the surgeons a little earlier, and honestly, we should have tested the console one more time before the first case. That is my one suggestion: flag the risks sooner. I am not blaming anyone. I would have done the same under that pressure. I will be honest but kind, always. I acknowledge how much effort this took, and I am happy to address any questions or hear your perspective. Thank you again for your great work.

Let me describe how Andreia gives her feedback. First, she praises a real strength: the team's clear communication. Then she shares her perspective and offers one suggestion. Next, she softens the difficult part with a past modal: you could have explained the risk earlier. After that, she stays honest but kind, and acknowledges the team's effort. Finally, she invites their perspective and offers to address any questions.
